serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Sauteed Shredded Pork in Sweet Bean Sauce is approximately 150 g, representing a single-person main dish portion served alongside pancakes or tofu skin.

nutrient_source.title: Sauteed Shredded Pork in Sweet Bean Sauce — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010136). gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Jing Jiang Rou Si. The dish as recorded by HPB contains 0 g/100g carbohydrate, yielding a glycemic load of 0 per serving. A nominal GI of 35 (low) is assigned by analogy to soy-sauce-based pork stir-fry dishes with negligible carbohydrate content, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for low-carbohydrate savoury meat dishes. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (35 × 0.0 × 1.50) / 100 = 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold; potassium at 47 mg/100 g provides no meaningful offset.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 1575 mg sodium, representing over 68% of the 2300 mg daily limit, and the very low potassium-to-sodium ratio further impairs blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 0 g/100 g and sugar 0 g/100 g as recorded; high protein (19.1 g/100 g) supports satiety without raising blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish itself carries negligible glycaemic load, making it suitable for blood glucose management, but patients must account for the carbohydrate in accompanying pancakes or tofu skin wrappers, which can significantly raise the meal's glycaemic load; confirm sweet bean sauce brand for variable sugar content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 1050 mg/100 g poses a direct cardiovascular risk; cholesterol at 89 mg/100 g and unquantified saturated fat from pork loin and cooking oil add further atherogenic burden.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its very high sodium content and moderate cholesterol load; if consumed, portion size should be restricted to no more than 50–75 g and paired with high-potassium vegetables to partially offset sodium impact.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying inflammatory cytokine pathways; sodium at 1050 mg/100 g may exacerbate joint swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; the pro-inflammatory omega-6 fatty acid profile of pork combined with very high sodium can worsen joint inflammation and tissue oedema — substituting with chicken breast or tofu is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sauteed Shredded Pork in Sweet Bean Sauce (京酱肉丝, Jīng jiàng ròu sī) is a classic Northern Chinese dish originating from Beijing cuisine. It is prepared by marinating thin strips of pork loin in soy sauce and starch, then stir-frying them at high heat with tianmianjiang (sweet bean sauce / sweet flour paste), a fermented wheat-based condiment that gives the dish its characteristic deep umami-sweet flavour and dark mahogany colour. The dish is traditionally served with julienned spring onions, shredded cucumber, and either tofu skin rolls or thin handmade pancakes used as wraps. The texture is tender and slightly glossy, with a savoury-sweet sauce coating each strand of pork. Per 100 g, the HPB Singapore Foodo database records 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and 0 g carbohydrate — reflecting the dish without its accompaniments. Sodium is very high at 1050 mg/100 g, driven by sweet bean sauce and soy sauce, making this dish unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. Cholesterol is 89 mg/100 g. The dish is low in carbohydrate and has negligible glycaemic impact on its own, making it conditionally suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when accompaniments are controlled. It is not suitable for halal or kosher diets. In Singapore, this dish appears on the menus of Beijing-style Chinese restaurants and is popular as a lunch or dinner main course.

GI is an analogous estimate (low, ~35) as no direct GI measurement exists for this dish; glycemic load per 150 g serving is 0.0 given 0 g carbohydrate. The dish is not halal due to pork content. Accompaniments (pancakes, tofu skin) are not included in the per_100g nutrient values.
